title: The Crippled Snake's Secret
content:
The Ceremonial Grounds of Thousand Beast Valley stretched across a plateau of white jade, where three thousand young disciples stood in perfect formation beneath a sky the color of bruised plums. Lin Xiao's palms were slick with sweat as he watched the elders file onto the central dais, their robes embroidered with beasts that seemed to move in the corner of your eye—serpents coiling, wolves stalking, eagles diving.

He'd waited sixteen years for this moment.

"Today, you become beast masters," Elder Shen announced, his voice carrying across the plateau without effort, the mark of a cultivator at the fourth stage of the Beast Heart Realm. "The bond you form this morning will shape your entire path of cultivation. Choose wisely. Cherish deeply. The beast that answers your call will be your partner for life."

Lin Xiao's heart hammered against his ribs. He'd memorized every beast manual in the sect's library, studied the strengths and weaknesses of every known spirit beast in the valley. He knew the lightning hawk could sense qi fluctuations from three miles away. He knew the shadow panther could merge with darkness itself. He knew the iron-backed bear could shatter boulders with a single swipe.

He wanted any of them. All of them. But he'd settle for one.

"First formation, step forward!"

The disciples ahead of him moved with the confidence of those who'd already won. Lin Xiao watched as a girl from the Azure Peak branch summoned a crystal fox, its fur shimmering like morning frost. A boy from the same branch called forth a bronze-scaled serpent that wrapped around his arm like living armor. Each successful bond drew murmurs of approval from the watching elders.

Lin Xiao's stomach tightened.

"Twenty-third formation!"

His turn.

He stepped onto the central platform, and the world narrowed to a single point—the summoning circle carved into the jade before him. It pulsed with a faint blue light, waiting for his qi to activate it. Behind him, he could feel the eyes of his fellow disciples. He knew what they were thinking. Lin Xiao, the boy from the Southern Slopes who'd barely passed his qi foundation exam. Lin Xiao, who'd never tamed so much as a field mouse.

*Prove them wrong*, he told himself.

He pressed his palm to the circle and poured his qi into it.

The light flared. The air crackled. And then—

Nothing.

The circle sputtered. Flickered. Died.

"Again," Elder Shen said, his voice flat.

Lin Xiao tried again. More qi this time, drawing from the deepest reserves of his core. The circle blazed to life, brighter than before, and shapes began to form within it. A serpent. Yes, a serpent—he could see the outline of its body, the curve of its fangs. His heart soared.

Then the light faded.

And the serpent that remained was nothing like the bronze-scaled beast he'd imagined.

It was barely two feet long. Its scales were a dull, sickly gray, cracked in a dozen places. One eye was clouded white. The other was the color of old blood. It lay curled in the center of the summoning circle, too weak to lift its head, and when it tried to hiss, only a pathetic wheeze escaped its throat.

Laughter rippled through the crowd.

"Is that... a wind-snake?"

"Looks like it's missing a few parts."

"Lin Xiao bonded with a cripple. What a joke."

Elder Shen's face was unreadable. "A spirit beast with a broken core," he said, his voice carrying no judgment but also no comfort. "It will never grow beyond its current stage. You may release the bond and attempt again in one year."

One year. A full year of humiliation, of watching his peers advance while he stagnated. A year of being the laughingstock of the valley.

Lin Xiao looked down at the snake.

It met his gaze with its one good eye. And in that eye, he saw something that made him pause. Not fear. Not pain. Something else. Something that looked almost like... recognition.

"Master Lin?" Elder Shen prompted. "Your decision?"

"I'll keep it."

The words left his mouth before he could stop them. The crowd's laughter turned to gasps, then to whispers. Even Elder Shen seemed taken aback.

"You understand what this means," the elder said slowly. "A broken-core beast cannot cultivate. It will never evolve. You will be bound to a companion that offers you no cultivation benefits, no battle prowess, no—"

"I understand." Lin Xiao knelt and extended his hand to the snake. "But I chose it. And it chose me."

The snake's tongue flickered out, touching his fingers. A spark of warmth traveled up his arm, and he felt the bond snap into place—a thread of silver light connecting his heart to the beast's. It was thin. Fragile. A bond that would never strengthen.

But it was his.

"Very well." Elder Shen turned away. "The ceremony continues. Next formation!"

Lin Xiao stepped off the platform, the snake coiled weakly around his wrist, and walked through a gauntlet of stares. Some were pitying. Most were mocking. A few—from the older disciples, the ones who'd been through this themselves—were simply curious.

*What kind of idiot keeps a broken beast?*

He didn't have an answer for them. He barely had an answer for himself.

---

The disciples' dormitory was a long stone building divided into small private rooms, each barely large enough for a bed and a desk. Lin Xiao's room was at the far end, furthest from the dining hall and closest to the latrines. A room for the lowest-ranked disciple.

He laid the snake on his bed and examined it properly for the first time.

Up close, it was worse than he'd thought. The cracks in its scales weren't just surface damage—they went deep, exposing the muscle beneath. The clouded eye was completely blind. And when he pressed his fingers gently against its body, he felt the broken core—a shattered crystal no bigger than a grain of rice, rattling loose inside its chest cavity.

"Great," he muttered. "I bonded with a corpse that hasn't had the decency to die yet."

The snake's tongue flickered. It seemed almost... amused.

"You can understand me, can't you?" Lin Xiao asked. Even a crippled wind-snake should have enough intelligence for basic communication. "Is there anything you can do? Anything at all?"

The snake stared at him.

Then, slowly, it opened its mouth.

And spoke.

"You have... interesting... qi."

Lin Xiao nearly fell off his bed. "You can talk?"

"All... spirit beasts can talk." The snake's voice was a whisper, each word separated by a long pause. "Most choose not to. Humans... talk too much."

"I've never heard of a wind-snake that can speak."

"Wind-snakes can't." The snake's eye glinted. "But I am not... a wind-snake."

Lin Xiao's heart stopped. "What do you mean?"

"What do you know... of the Thousand Beast Valley's... founding?"

The question caught him off guard. "I know it was established three thousand years ago by the Beast Saint, who tamed the first spirit beasts and taught humans how to bond with them."

"Good. What else?"

"He created the summoning circles. Established the laws of bonding. Wrote the—"

"He wrote nothing." The snake's voice grew stronger, each word less pained. "The Beast Saint's teachings were destroyed in the Purge Wars. Everything you know... is a reconstruction. Incomplete. Wrong."

Lin Xiao's mind raced. "How do you know this?"

"Because I was there."

The words hit him like a physical blow. "You're saying you're three thousand years old?"

"Older." The snake shifted, and for a moment, Lin Xiao saw something flicker beneath its dull scales—a pattern of light, like constellations swirling in a dark sky. "I was the Beast Saint's first bond. His most powerful. His... mistake."

"Mistake?"

"I was too strong. The other elders grew afraid. They conspired to destroy me." The snake's voice turned bitter. "They broke my core. Sealed me in a pocket dimension. Left me to rot for millennia... until your summoning circle cracked the seal."

Lin Xiao's head was spinning. "So you're... an ancient beast? A legendary creature?"

"Once. Now I am what you see. A crippled snake with a broken core and a few words left to speak before I fade entirely."

"And you bonded with me because...?"

"Because your qi resonated with mine." The snake's eye met his. "Because you saw me for what I am, not what I appear to be. And because..." It paused. "Because I have one last gift to give before I die."

"What gift?"

"Beast Resonance."

The words hung in the air like a promise. Or a curse.

"I've never heard of it," Lin Xiao said.

"Because it was forbidden. Erased from history. The Beast Saint created it, and the elders destroyed all records of it after they destroyed me." The snake's body began to glow, a faint silver light that pulsed in rhythm with Lin Xiao's heartbeat. "Beast Resonance allows a master and beast to merge their essences. To combine their powers. To become... more than the sum of their parts."

"You mean we can—"

"Fuse. Yes." The snake's voice was barely a whisper now. "But it comes at a cost. Each merge drains my remaining life force. I have perhaps... seven uses left. Seven chances for you to learn the technique before I am gone."

Lin Xiao felt tears prick at his eyes. "Seven?"

"Seven is enough." The snake's head drooped. "I have taught kings and tyrants, saints and sinners. But you... you kept me when others would have thrown me away. That is worth more than a thousand years of power."

"What do I do?"

"Close your eyes. Open your core. And listen."

Lin Xiao obeyed. He felt the snake's qi intertwine with his own, a thread of silver winding through the red-gold of his cultivation base. It was painful—like having his insides rearranged—but beneath the pain was something else. A warmth. A connection.

"Now," the snake whispered, "let me show you what it means to truly bond with a beast."

The world dissolved.

Lin Xiao found himself standing in a void of silver light. And in that void, he saw the snake—not as it was, but as it had been. A titan of scales and shadow, a creature that could swallow mountains and drink rivers dry. Its true form stretched across the void, infinite and terrible.

"This is what I was," the snake said. "And this is what you can become. If you dare."

"How?" Lin Xiao breathed.

"By mastering Beast Resonance. By merging with other beasts. By collecting their essences and making them your own." The snake's eye softened. "Your talent is rare, Lin Xiao. One in a billion. The Beast Saint himself only found three Resonators in his entire lifetime."

"And you'll teach me?"

"I will try." The snake's form began to fade. "But my time is short. You must find other beasts. Strong beasts. Bond with them. Learn their essences. And when the time comes, you must merge them all."

"Merge them all?"

"Seven beasts. Seven essences. Seven resonances." The snake's voice was fading. "That is the secret the elders tried to destroy. That is the path to true power."

Lin Xiao opened his eyes.

He was back in his dormitory, the snake coiled weakly on his chest. The silver light was gone. The warmth was fading

title: The Warden of the Forgotten Pit
content:
The pit stank of rot and despair.

Lin Xiao pressed his back against the damp stone wall, his lungs burning with each breath. The crippled wind-snake coiled around his wrist, its scales cold and lifeless. Above him, the circle of torchlight seemed impossibly far—fifty feet, maybe more. The Warden had thrown him here without a word, without a trial. Just a boot to the chest and the grinding sound of the iron grate slamming shut.

"You're dead, boy," a voice rasped from the darkness.

Lin Xiao's head snapped up. He couldn't see anything beyond the faint green glow of his own beast core—that pathetic, flickering mote of light that marked him as the weakest beast master in Thousand Beast Valley's history. The core pulsed weakly against his sternum, barely illuminating the grime-caked floor.

"Who's there?"

A wet laugh echoed through the pit. "Someone who made the same mistake you did. Trusted the wrong person. Challenged the wrong master."

The wind-snake stirred, its forked tongue brushing against Lin Xiao's thumb. *Danger,* it whispered into his mind—not with words, but with sensations. A beast master's bond worked that way when it was deep enough. Feelings, images, instincts. The snake had been broken for years, its core shattered by a hunting accident before Lin Xiao had even been born. But it still remembered how to fear.

"I'm not dead yet," Lin Xiao said, forcing steel into his voice.

"Yet." The voice chuckled. "That's the key word. The Warden doesn't throw people into the Forgotten Pit to have tea with them. This is where beast masters come to die. Slowly. Quietly. Until their cores go dark and their beasts abandon them."

Lin Xiao's hand drifted to his belt pouch. The lightning hawk feather was still there, tucked against his hip. He'd found it yesterday, moments before everything went wrong—a single iridescent feather, crackling with residual lightning energy, lying in the mud of the lower training grounds. He'd picked it up on instinct, feeling the faint pulse of the beast that had shed it.

In the distance, a bell tolled. Once. Twice. Three times.

"That's the evening watch," the voice said. "You've got until dawn. After that, the Warden sends in the scavengers."

"Scavengers?"

"Beasts that feed on broken cores. They'll smell yours like blood in water. And when they come—" The voice paused, and Lin Xiao heard the rattle of chains. "When they come, boy, you'd better hope you've made peace with whatever gods you worship."

Lin Xiao closed his eyes. His mother's face floated in his memory—that final look of disappointment before she'd turned her back on him. "A crippled snake for a crippled son," she'd said. "At least you match."

He'd believed her. For sixteen years, he'd believed her.

Then yesterday happened.

The lightning hawk feather pulsed against his fingers. Lin Xiao pulled it out, watching the faint blue sparks dance along its edges. The wind-snake raised its head, its broken eyes fixed on the feather.

*Hungry,* it sent.

"No," Lin Xiao whispered. "Not hungry. Resonant."

The word felt strange on his tongue. He'd never heard anyone else use it. The [Beast Resonance] talent had awakened in him during the attack—that moment when the Shadow Panther had lunged at him, and something had *clicked* inside his chest. For half a heartbeat, he'd felt the panther's thoughts, its instincts, its *power* flowing through him like a river of midnight silk.

But he didn't have the panther anymore. He had a crippled snake and a feather.

And a pit full of darkness and death.

"I need to get out," Lin Xiao muttered.

"Everyone needs to get out," the voice said. "Nobody does."

Lin Xiao ignored him. He pressed the feather against his beast core—that pitiful, flickering light—and concentrated. He didn't know what he was doing. No one had taught him. The valley's masters taught cultivation through meditation, through breathing techniques, through the slow accumulation of qi. They taught nothing about *resonance*.

But Lin Xiao had felt it. That connection. That merging.

He closed his eyes and *pushed*.

The feather grew hot. Then cold. Then it *dissolved* into his skin, and Lin Xiao gasped as lightning flooded his veins.

"WHAT WAS THAT?" the voice shouted.

Lin Xiao couldn't answer. His body was no longer his own.

The lightning hawk's memories crashed into him like a tidal wave—the feeling of riding thermals above the valley, the scent of rain on distant mountains, the *ecstasy* of a dive at terminal velocity. He felt its hunger, its loneliness, its rage at being trapped in a cage too small for its wings.

And he felt its power.

The beast core in his chest flared. Not the weak, flickering light of before—but a brilliant, blinding *blue*. Lightning arced across his skin, dancing between his fingers, crackling in his hair. The wind-snake *screamed*—a high-pitched, keening sound—and wrapped itself around his arm so tightly he felt its scales cutting into his flesh.

*YES,* the snake sent. *YES YES YES.*

Lin Xiao opened his eyes.

The pit was no longer dark. He could see everything—the slick stone walls, the bones scattered across the floor, the figure in chains huddled against the far corner. The man was gaunt, bearded, his beast core barely a dim ember in his chest. He stared at Lin Xiao with wide, disbelieving eyes.

"How—" the man started.

Lin Xiao didn't answer. He was too busy understanding.

The lightning hawk's power was *his* now. Not borrowed. Not channeled. *His*. The feather had been shed weeks ago, but the beast's essence had lingered in it, waiting for someone who could receive it. Someone with [Beast Resonance].

Someone like him.

He looked up at the grate fifty feet above. The bars were iron, thick as his wrist, spaced just close enough to keep a man from squeezing through. But a man with lightning in his blood?

"Stay back," Lin Xiao said.

"What?"

"I said stay back."

He raised his hand, and lightning *answered*.

The bolt that erupted from his palm was not gentle. It was not controlled. It was raw, primal, *angry*—the fury of a caged hawk finally given wings. It struck the grate with a thunderclap that shook the pit, sending chunks of molten iron raining down around them.

The man in chains screamed and covered his head.

Lin Xiao fired again. And again. And again.

Each bolt was weaker than the last. The lightning hawk's power was finite—a single feather's worth of essence, not the full beast itself. He could feel it draining, the brilliant blue light in his core dimming with every discharge.

But it was enough.

On the fourth bolt, the grate shattered. Iron fragments clattered against the walls, and a circle of moonlight appeared above—the first light Lin Xiao had seen since they'd thrown him in.

"Impossible," the chained man whispered.

Lin Xiao turned to him. The man's eyes were wet. Not with tears—with *hope*. The desperate, terrible hope of someone who had given up decades ago and was suddenly forced to believe again.

"Who are you?" the man asked.

"Lin Xiao. Son of Lin Feng. Beast master of Thousand Beast Valley."

"No." The man shook his head. "That's not what I meant. *What* are you?"

Lin Xiao didn't have an answer. He could feel the lightning hawk's power fading, the essence almost spent. In a few minutes, he'd be back to his crippled snake and his flickering core.

But for now, he was something else.

"I don't know," he said honestly. "But I'm going to find out."

The man's chains rattled as he tried to stand. He couldn't—his legs were too weak, his muscles too atrophied from years in the dark. "Take me with you."

"I don't even know who you are."

"My name is Shen Wei. I was the Warden's apprentice. I know his secrets. His weaknesses. I know why he threw you in here." The man's voice cracked. "I know what he's *building*."

Lin Xiao hesitated. The wind-snake tightened around his arm, sending a pulse of warning. *Danger,* it sent again. *Lies.*

But the man's core was broken. He had no beasts left—Lin Xiao could sense their absence like missing teeth in a smile. He was no threat.

"Talk fast," Lin Xiao said.

Shen Wei licked his cracked lips. "The Warden isn't just a beast master. He's a *collector*. He's been hunting the valley's rarest beasts for years, caging them, breeding them, trying to create—"

A footstep scraped above them.

Lin Xiao looked up. A silhouette stood at the edge of the broken grate, backlit by the moon. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Wreathed in a cloak that seemed to drink the light.

"Lin Xiao," the Warden said, his voice a low rumble. "I was wondering when you'd stop pretending to be weak."

The lightning hawk's essence was almost gone. Lin Xiao could feel it flickering, dying, leaving him empty.

"I'm not weak," he said.

"No." The Warden's teeth gleamed in the darkness. "You're not. You're something far more interesting." He reached into his cloak and pulled out a cage—small, iron, humming with power. Inside, a creature stirred. Lin Xiao couldn't see it clearly, but he felt its presence. It was vast. Hungry. *Ancient*.

"You see," the Warden said, "I've been waiting for someone like you. Someone with the Resonance. The ability to merge with beasts." He smiled. "Do you know what happens when you merge a god with a mortal?"

The cage door swung open.

And the world went black.

When Lin Xiao's vision returned, he was on his knees. The pit was silent except for the ragged sound of his own breathing. Shen Wei was gone—the chains lay empty on the floor, the man vanished like smoke.

Above him, the Warden stood alone.

"Where—"

"Gone," the Warden said. "He served his purpose." He crouched at the edge of the broken grate, studying Lin Xiao like a merchant appraising livestock. "You have potential, boy. Raw. Unrefined. But potential nonetheless."

"What did you do to me?"

"I gave you a gift." The Warden's smile widened. "A parasite. It will live in your core, feeding on your cultivation, growing stronger with every beast you bond. And when it's ready..." He stood, turning away. "When it's ready, I'll come back for it."

Lin Xiao tried to stand. His legs wouldn't obey. He tried to call lightning—nothing. The wind-snake was limp around his arm, barely breathing.

"You can't fight it," the Warden said without looking back. "Resistance only makes it grow faster. So go ahead. Struggle. Bond with beasts. Grow stronger." He laughed, a cold, hollow sound. "Every step you take toward power is a step closer to me."

His boots echoed against the stone as he walked away.

Lin Xiao collapsed face-first into the mud, the taste of blood and failure filling his mouth. Something stirred in his core—a presence that wasn't his, a hunger that wasn't his own

title: The Shadow Panther's Test
content:
The morning sun painted Thousand Beast Valley in hues of amber and gold, but Lin Xiao felt none of its warmth. Three days had passed since his breakthrough to the second level of the Beast Melding Realm, and his body still hummed with unfamiliar energy. The broken wind-snake coiled around his left arm, its scales cool against his skin, while the lightning hawk perched on his shoulder, preening feathers that crackled with residual sparks.

"You're pushing yourself too hard," a voice said from behind him.

Lin turned to find Elder Wu standing at the entrance of his crude hut, the old man's weathered face creased with concern. Despite his age, Elder Wu moved with the fluid grace of someone who had spent decades communing with beasts. His bonded companion, a massive earth bear, lumbered at his side, its fur caked with dried mud.

"I need to be ready," Lin replied, clenching his fist. "The sect trials are in two weeks."

"And you think rushing into the Shadowmire without proper preparation is wise?"

Lin's jaw tightened. The Shadowmire—a swamp in the eastern section of the valley where shadow-attuned beasts made their dens. It was the only place he could find a shadow panther, and his plan required one.

"I don't have time to waste."

Elder Wu sighed, shaking his head. "Your [Beast Resonance] is a rare gift, boy. But gifts don't make up for stupidity. What exactly are you planning?"

Lin hesitated, then extended his hand. The lightning hawk chirped, fluttering down to perch on his palm. With a thought, Lin activated his ability.

*[Beast Resonance: Active]*

The world shifted. Colors bled together, sounds became sharper, and he felt the hawk's instincts overlay his own. His vision sharpened, picking out the faint heat signatures of small creatures in the underbrush. The wind-snake's head lifted, its tongue flickering as it tasted the air through their shared senses.

Elder Wu's eyes widened. "You can maintain the link with two beasts simultaneously?"

"Three, if I push it," Lin said, releasing the resonance. The world snapped back to normal, leaving him slightly dizzy. "But it's not enough. When I fought those Sect disciples, I barely won. If they'd had their bonded beasts with them, I would have lost."

"So you want a shadow panther to add stealth and ambush capabilities to your arsenal."

"Exactly. With the hawk's ranged attacks, the snake's poison, and the panther's shadow-walking, I can cover every combat scenario."

Elder Wu was silent for a long moment. Then he reached into his robes and pulled out a jade talisman, tossing it to Lin. "Take this. It's a beast-repelling charm. Won't work on anything above the third rank, but it'll keep the lesser creatures off your back."

Lin caught the talisman, warmth spreading through his chest. "Why are you helping me?"

"Because I've seen what happens to talented boys who think they're invincible," Elder Wu said, his voice heavy. "They end up as fertilizer for the valley. Don't become another cautionary tale."

The old man turned and walked away, the earth bear following close behind. Lin watched him go, then looked down at the talisman in his hand. For the first time in days, he allowed himself a small smile.

---

The Shadowmire lived up to its name.

Dark, twisted trees rose from murky water, their roots creating treacherous walkways that shifted with every step. Mist hung thick in the air, muffling sounds and distorting distances. Lin moved carefully, the wind-snake wrapped around his neck like a living scarf, its tongue constantly tasting for threats.

The lightning hawk circled overhead, its cry echoing through the swamp. Through their link, Lin could feel its irritation—the mist interfered with its vision, making it difficult to spot prey from above.

*"Stay alert,"* he sent through the resonance. *"We're not alone."*

Something moved in the shadows to his left. Lin froze, his hand inching toward the dagger at his belt. The wind-snake's coils tightened, a warning.

A pair of golden eyes blinked into existence twenty feet away.

Then another pair. And another.

Lin counted six sets of eyes before he stopped. Shadow beasts. Not panthers, but lesser shadow-foxes. Pack hunters that used the mist to disorient their prey before striking.

*"Hawk, light them up."*

The lightning hawk shrieked, diving from the sky. Lightning erupted from its wings, illuminating the swamp in a strobe of white-blue light. The shadow-foxes hissed, their eyes adjusting painfully to the sudden brightness.

Lin moved.

He wasn't as fast as the beasts, but he didn't need to be. The wind-snake uncoiled, launching itself at the nearest fox. Its fangs sank into the creature's neck, venom flooding the wound. The fox yelped, stumbling back as its muscles seized.

Two more foxes circled, trying to flank him. Lin spun, his dagger flashing. The blade caught one across the snout, drawing blood. The other lunged, jaws aiming for his throat.

*[Beast Resonance: Merge]*

Time slowed.

Lin felt the wind-snake's instincts flood his mind—the way its body moved without bones, the way it sensed vibrations through the air. His spine seemed to liquefy as he twisted, flowing around the fox's attack like water around a stone. His hand shot out, fingers clenched into a spear, and drove into the beast's exposed ribs.

The fox yelped, scrambling back. But Lin didn't let up. He pressed forward, striking again and again, his movements becoming more serpentine with each blow. The wind-snake's essence sang through his veins, amplifying his speed and flexibility beyond human limits.

By the time the remaining foxes fled, Lin stood panting over three corpses. The resonance faded, leaving him feeling drained but exhilarated.

*"Good work,"* he sent to his beasts.

The lightning hawk landed on his shoulder, nuzzling his cheek. The wind-snake slithered back up his arm, its tongue flickering contentedly.

But there was no time to rest. The commotion would attract larger predators. Lin forced himself to move deeper into the swamp, his eyes scanning for any sign of shadow panther tracks.

---

He found them near a collapsed stone structure—what looked like the ruins of an ancient temple, half-submerged in the murky water. Pugmarks, easily twice the size of his hand, led into the darkness beneath the crumbling archway.

Lin's heart raced. This was it.

He approached cautiously, activating his [Beast Sight]—a basic ability that allowed beast tamers to sense the spiritual energy of nearby creatures. The temple pulsed with dark qi, thick and cloying. A shadow panther's den, no doubt.

*"Wait here,"* he told his beasts. *"If I don't come out in fifteen minutes, come find me."*

The lightning hawk chirped in protest, but Lin was already moving into the darkness.

The air grew cold as he descended into the temple's depths. Strange carvings lined the walls—depictions of ancient beast tamers communing with shadow spirits, their forms merging into twisted shapes that defied nature. Lin ignored them, his focus fixed on the path ahead.

The tunnel opened into a large chamber, and Lin stopped dead.

A shadow panther lay in the center of the room, its sleek black form curled around a pile of bones. But this wasn't an ordinary shadow panther. Its fur rippled with living darkness, and its eyes—when they opened—bore the unmistakable gleam of intelligence.

*"You reek of other beasts, little tamer,"* a voice echoed in his mind. Not words, but impressions. Feelings. The panther's spiritual pressure pressed down on him, making his knees tremble. *"Why have you come?"*

Lin swallowed. This beast was at least third rank—maybe higher. A single mistake and he'd be dead.

"I seek a bond," he said, keeping his voice steady. "I need your strength."

The panther rose, its movements fluid and predatory. It circled him, its tail swishing lazily. *"You barely control the beasts you have. Why would I submit to one so weak?"*

"Because I can offer you something no one else can."

The panther stopped, tilting its head. *"And what is that?"*

Lin extended his hand, activating [Beast Resonance]. The panther's eyes widened as it felt the strange energy wash over it—the promise of fusion, of becoming something greater than a single entity.

*"I can make you stronger,"* Lin said. *"Stronger than any beast in this valley. Together, we can reach heights no beast tamer has ever achieved."*

For a long moment, the panther was silent. Then, slowly, it stepped forward and pressed its forehead against Lin's palm.

The bond formed like fire in his chest.

*[Shadow Panther Bonded: Rank 3 - Shadowmire Stalker]*

Lin gasped as the panther's power flooded his meridians, pushing his cultivation toward the third level of the Beast Melding Realm. His vision doubled, then tripled, as he saw through the panther's eyes—darkness no longer an obstacle, but a cloak.

*"We have a deal, little tamer,"* the panther's voice echoed in his mind. *"But know this—I will test you. And if you prove unworthy, I will devour your soul."*

Lin smiled, despite the threat. "Fair enough."

---

He emerged from the temple an hour later, the shadow panther padding silently at his side. The lightning hawk shrieked in alarm at the new arrival, but settled when Lin sent calming thoughts through their bond.

*"Three beasts,"* Lin murmured, looking at his hands. The power thrumming through his veins was intoxicating. *"I actually have three beasts."*

But the celebration was short-lived.

A scream echoed through the swamp—high-pitched and terrified. Lin's head snapped up, his new senses extending outward. He felt them: a group of people, surrounded by something big. Something angry.

*"Come on,"* he said, breaking into a sprint. The shadow panther vanished into the darkness, its presence becoming a whisper in his mind. The lightning hawk took to the air, scouting ahead. The wind-snake coiled tighter, ready to strike.

He burst through a wall of mist and found chaos.

A hunting party—disciples from one of the major sects, judging by their robes—were pinned against a rock formation by a massive creature. A corrupted beast, its body a mess of twisted flesh and broken bone, stood twenty feet tall. Its eyes burned with malevolent red light, and black qi leaked from every wound on its body.

Corruption. The plague that had been spreading through Thousand Beast Valley for months.

"Help us!" one of the disciples cried, seeing Lin. "Please!"

Lin didn't hesitate. He raised his hand, and the shadow panther materialized behind the corrupted beast, claws extended. The lightning hawk dove, electricity arcing between its wings. The wind-snake hissed, venom dripping from its fangs.

*[Beast Resonance: Triple Merge]*

The world exploded into sensation.

Lin felt himself become something beyond human. The panther's stealth, the hawk's speed, the snake's flexibility—all of it flowed through him in a symphony of power. His body flickered with shadows, lightning sparked along his arms, and his eyes gleamed with serpentine cunning.

The corrupted beast roared, charging at him.

Lin smiled.

He moved like smoke, appearing behind the creature before it could react.

title: The Corruption's Source
content:
Lin's fist connected with the corrupted beast's spine.

The impact sent shockwaves through his arm, but the triple-merged power coursing through his veins absorbed the recoil. Bone cracked beneath his knuckles. The creature howled, stumbling forward as black ichor sprayed from the wound.

*"Behind you,"* the shadow panther's voice whispered.

Lin twisted, shadows carrying him sideways as a massive claw swept through where he'd been standing. The corrupted beast recovered faster than he expected, its broken body moving with unnatural fluidity. The red eyes tracked him with predatory intelligence that didn't match the mindless rage he'd seen in lesser corrupted beasts.

"Everyone get back!" Lin shouted at the pinned disciples. "Form a defensive circle!"

Five disciples scrambled to comply, their own beasts—a mix of rank 2 creatures—forming a protective ring around them. None looked strong enough to handle a beast of this caliber. Their trembling hands and pale faces told Lin everything he needed to know about their combat experience.

The lightning hawk circled above, azure bolts crackling between its talons. *"Its core is exposed,"* it shrieked into Lin's mind. *"Lower abdomen. The corruption hasn't fully sealed it."*

Lin saw it now—a faint glow beneath the twisted flesh, pulsing like a heartbeat. The creature's original beast core, struggling against the corruption that had consumed it.

*[Beast Resonance: Shadow Stride]*

He pushed the panther's power to the forefront, feeling his body dissolve into living darkness. The corrupted beast's claws passed through him harmlessly as he flowed beneath its guard. His hand reformed inches from the glowing core, fingers extending like blades.

He struck.

The moment his fingers touched the core, everything stopped.

Visions flooded his mind—not his own, but the beast's. A young ironhide bear, playing in a stream with its siblings. A flash of darkness descending from the sky. A man in black robes, his face obscured by a hood, pressing a palm to the bear's forehead. Black qi pouring into the beast's core, twisting it, breaking it, remaking it into something monstrous.

And then the man turned.

He looked directly at Lin.

*"Tell the elders,"* the man's voice echoed, ancient and cold. *"Tell them their end is coming. Tell them—"*

The vision shattered.

Lin gasped, stumbling backward as the corrupted beast roared in agony. The core in its belly cracked, releasing a wave of black energy that sent him flying. He crashed through a dead tree, splinters cutting his arms and face.

The beast collapsed, its body dissolving into black mist that dissolved into the swamp air. Where it had stood, only a cracked, smoking core remained, its light flickering weakly before going dark.

Silence fell over the clearing.

Lin pushed himself up, his triple-merged form flickering as exhaustion crept in. The disciples stared at him with wide eyes, their beasts lowering their heads in submission.

"Who... who are you?" one of them stammered.

Lin ignored the question. His mind was still reeling from the vision. The man in black robes. The deliberate corruption. The message meant for the elders.

*"That was no random attack,"* the shadow panther said, materializing beside him. *"Someone sent that beast. Someone with purpose."*

"Someone with a grudge," Lin agreed.

The lightning hawk landed on his shoulder, its feathers still crackling with residual energy. The wind-snake uncoiled from around his waist, tasting the air with its forked tongue.

"We should get back to the village," Lin said. "Fast."

---

The journey back took half the time it should have.

With the shadow panther's guidance through the darkest parts of the swamp, and the lightning hawk's aerial reconnaissance, Lin navigated terrain that would have taken him hours in a matter of minutes. The disciples followed in stunned silence, their own beasts struggling to keep pace with Lin's merged speed.

By the time they reached the outer walls of the Thousand Beast Valley village, Lin's cultivation had stabilized at the peak of the second level of the Beast Melding Realm. The triple merge had pushed him further than he'd ever gone, and the aftermath left his meridians humming with untapped potential.

The village gates opened as they approached, guards recognizing the sect robes of the disciples Lin had rescued. An elder emerged from the crowd—Elder Huo, a stern-faced man with a rank 4 flame tiger at his side.

"Lin Xiao?" Elder Huo's eyes narrowed. "What happened?"

Lin stepped forward, his three beasts arrayed behind him like a living shadow. "Corrupted beast attack. A hunting party from the Crimson Flame Sect was pinned down. I eliminated the threat."

The elder's gaze swept over the disciples, noting their pale faces and trembling beasts. His expression shifted from suspicion to grudging respect.

"Impressive. Especially for someone whose bonded beast was a crippled wind-snake."

Lin felt the barb, but let it slide. "The corruption isn't random, Elder Huo. I saw the beast's memories before it died. A man in black robes deliberately infected it. He knew what he was doing."

The elder's face went still. "You saw this?"

"Clear as I see you now."

A murmur rippled through the gathered crowd. Elder Huo raised a hand for silence.

"Inside. Now. The Council needs to hear this."

---

The Council chamber was carved from the heart of an ancient beastwood tree, its walls etched with the names of every bonded beast that had served the valley for generations. Seven elders sat in a semicircle, their beasts positioned behind them like living thrones.

Lin stood in the center, his three beasts flanking him. The shadow panther remained invisible to most eyes, but he felt its presence like a second skin.

"Repeat what you told Elder Huo," the Chief Elder commanded. She was a woman in her sixties, her face weathered by decades of beast taming, her companion a rank 6 celestial crane that watched Lin with unsettling intelligence.

Lin told them everything. The attack. The vision. The man in black robes and his message.

When he finished, silence stretched for a long moment.

"The Corruption Bearer," the Chief Elder finally said. "We thought he died fifty years ago."

"He didn't die," Elder Huo said grimly. "He's been waiting. Building power."

"And now he's making his move," another elder added. "Using corrupted beasts to test our defenses."

Lin listened, pieces clicking into place. "You know who he is."

The Chief Elder's eyes met his. "His name is Mo Tian. Once our greatest beast tamer. He discovered a technique to merge beasts with corrupted qi, creating monsters beyond natural rank limitations. The Council exiled him for his experiments. He vowed to return and destroy everything we built."

"He's been active for months," Lin said. "The corrupted beasts in the swamp—"

"Are his scouts," the Chief Elder finished. "We knew. But we didn't know where his base was. The vision you saw... did you catch any landmarks? Any location?"

Lin closed his eyes, replaying the memory. The stream. The siblings. The flash of darkness descending...

"Mountains," he said slowly. "Black mountains with no vegetation. And a tower. A bone-white tower at the peak."

The elders exchanged glances.

"The Deadbone Peaks," Elder Huo said. "Of course. The corrupted qi there would mask his activities perfectly."

"We can't assault the peaks directly," the Chief Elder said. "Not without more information. Mo Tian has had fifty years to prepare. He'll have traps, corrupted beasts, and who knows what else."

Lin stepped forward. "I can scout."

The elders turned to him.

"You're barely at the second level of the Beast Melding Realm," one of them scoffed. "Mo Tian's weakest corrupted beast would crush you."

Lin smiled. He let his triple-merged power flicker at the edges of his aura—shadows, lightning, and serpentine grace all swirling together.

"I bonded three beasts in two days," he said. "My crippled wind-snake now fights at rank 2. I can merge their powers in ways no beast tamer in this valley has ever attempted. I'm not asking for permission. I'm telling you what I'm going to do."

The Chief Elder studied him for a long moment. Then she laughed—a dry, rasping sound.

"You have his arrogance," she said. "Mo Tian's, I mean. But you also have something he never did."

"What's that?"

"Beasts that chose you willingly. Not through force. Not through corruption. Through genuine bond." She nodded slowly. "Go. Scout the Deadbone Peaks. Report back in three days. But Lin Xiao—" Her voice hardened. "If you die, don't expect us to retrieve your body."

Lin bowed. "I wouldn't dream of it."

---

He left the village at dusk, his three beasts moving in perfect synchronization around him.

The shadow panther led, its senses extended miles ahead. The lightning hawk circled above, scanning for threats. The wind-snake coiled around his arm, its tongue flicking constantly, tasting the air for traces of corrupted qi.

*"You're actually doing this,"* the panther's voice echoed in his mind. *"Walking into the den of a madman who's had fifty years to prepare."*

"Yep."

*"You're either incredibly brave or incredibly stupid."*

"Probably both."

*"I respect that."*

The Deadbone Peaks rose in the distance, their black slopes stark against the darkening sky. Lin felt the corrupted qi even from here—a greasy sensation against his skin, like walking through cobwebs.

Somewhere up there, Mo Tian was waiting.

And somewhere up there, Lin would find answers.

Not just about the corruption.

About himself.

*[Beast Resonance: Passive Shadow Cloak]*

He vanished into the darkness, becoming one with the night.

The hunt had begun.

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER 5: The Weight of Shadows

Lin's fist connected with the corrupted beast's spine.

The impact sent shockwaves through his arm, but the merged power of three beasts absorbed the recoil. Shadows clung to his skin like a second layer of armor, lightning crackled around his knuckles, and the wind-snake's instincts guided his movements with fluid precision.

The creature stumbled forward, its massive claws gouging trenches in the muddy ground. Black qi hissed from the wound Lin had opened, sizzling against the swamp air.

"Keep it off balance!" one of the trapped disciples shouted—a girl with a crimson robe and a broken sword in her hand.

Lin didn't need the advice. He was already moving, his body responding faster than thought. The shadow panther's presence whispered in his mind, showing him angles of attack the human eye couldn't perceive. The lightning hawk's vision gave him a bird's-eye view of the battlefield. The wind-snake's instincts warned him of every twitch in the corrupted beast's muscles.

*This is what [Beast Resonance] was meant to be,* Lin thought. *Not just merging with one beast. Merging with all of them.*

The corrupted beast roared, its twisted jaws opening wide. A torrent of black liquid shot toward Lin—corrupted qi given form, carrying the stench of decay.

Lin blurred sideways, shadows carrying him faster than any footwork could. The black stream missed him by inches, striking a tree behind him. The wood dissolved on contact, leaving nothing but a smoking stump.

"Watch out for its breath!" the crimson-robed girl shouted. "It melts everything!"

"Noted," Lin muttered.

He raised his hand, and the lightning hawk answered. Electricity gathered in his palm, condensing into a spear of pure white light. The wind-snake's venom mixed with the energy, giving it a greenish tint that pulsed with killing intent.

The corrupted beast turned, its red eyes locking onto Lin. It was faster than it looked—much faster. It closed the distance in three strides, its claws swiping at Lin's chest.

Lin didn't dodge.

He let the claws pass through him.

The shadow panther's ability—[Phase Shifting]—allowed him to become intangible for a fraction of a second. The claws sliced through empty air, and Lin appeared inside the creature's guard, the lightning spear already thrusting forward.

The weapon pierced the corrupted beast's chest, exploding outward in a web of electrical energy. Black qi erupted from the wound, spraying across the swamp. The creature screamed—a sound that was half beast, half something else. Something wrong.

*It's not just corrupted,* Lin realized. *It's been fused with something. Forcibly.*

He could feel it through the bond. The corrupted beast's core wasn't natural. It pulsed with an artificial rhythm, like a heartbeat that didn't belong to the world. Someone had created this thing.

Someone was experimenting.

The creature collapsed, its body dissolving into a puddle of black sludge. The disciples scrambled away from the mess, their faces pale with shock.

Lin released the merge, his body sagging with exhaustion. The lightning hawk landed on his shoulder, its feathers ruffled. The shadow panther materialized at his side, its golden eyes watching the disciples with undisguised suspicion. The wind-snake slithered up his arm, coiling around his neck.

"Thank you," the crimson-robed girl said, her voice shaky. "We would have died if you hadn't shown up. I'm Zhao Mei, senior disciple of the Crimson Flame Sect."

"Lin Xiao. Thousand Beast Valley."

Zhao Mei's eyes widened. "Thousand Beast Valley? But that's—" She stopped herself, but Lin heard the unspoken words. *That's where the failures go.*

"I know," he said, forcing a smile. "I'm the one with the crippled snake."

The wind-snake hissed in protest, but Lin patted its head gently. "No offense, little one."

"What did you do back there?" a male disciple asked, stepping forward. His robes marked him as a member of the Heavenly Sword Sect—the most prestigious sect in the region. His tone wasn't admiring. It was suspicious. "I've never seen a beast tamer move like that. Or merge with three beasts at once."

"Natural talent," Lin said.

The disciple's eyes narrowed. "Natural talent doesn't let a second-level Beast Melding cultivator kill a corrupted beast that required a team of fourth-level experts to subdue. What's your secret?"

*[Beast Resonance],* Lin thought. *But I'm not telling you that.*

"It's just a technique I developed," he said carefully. "Nothing special."

"We'll be the judge of that," the disciple said. "I'm Jin Chen, by the way. And I think you need to come with us."

"Why?"

Jin Chen gestured to the puddle of black sludge. "Because that's not the first corrupted beast we've encountered. It's the fifth this month. And they're getting stronger. More organized. Someone's breeding them. And anyone who can kill one like you just did might have answers we need."

"Or he's the one breeding them," Zhao Mei added quietly.

Lin's blood ran cold. "You think I'm responsible for this?"

"I don't know what you are," Jin Chen said. "But I'm not taking chances. You're coming with us to the Heavenly Sword Sect's compound. We'll sort this out there."

The shadow panther growled, its body lowering into a fighting stance. The lightning hawk's feathers bristled with electricity. The wind-snake's fangs gleamed.

Lin felt the urge to merge again, to show these arrogant disciples what he was truly capable of. But something held him back. A whisper from the shadow panther.

*Patience, little tamer. The bigger threat is not here.*

He looked at Jin Chen, then at Zhao Mei. Both of them were at least fourth-level Beast Melding Realm cultivators. Their beasts were probably even stronger. And there were a dozen other disciples behind them, all armed and ready.

"I'll go," Lin said. "But only because I want to know who's behind these attacks."

Jin Chen nodded curtly. "Smart choice."

They bound his hands with a qi-suppressing rope—standard procedure for "guests" who might be dangerous. The shadow panther vanished into the shadows, refusing to be restrained. The lightning hawk took to the sky, circling overhead. The wind-snake stayed coiled around Lin's neck, a silent guardian.

The journey to the Heavenly Sword Sect's compound took three hours through the swamp. Lin used the time to study his captors. Zhao Mei was clearly the leader of the hunting party, her crimson robes marking her as a senior disciple. Jin Chen was her second-in-command, his suspicion suggesting he'd seen too many betrayals to trust easily.

The other disciples kept their distance, whispering among themselves. Lin caught fragments of their conversation—"three beasts," "never seen anything like it," "could be the one the elders warned us about."

*The elders warned them about me?* Lin thought. *How is that possible? I'm nobody.*

They reached the compound as the sun began to set. It was a fortress carved into the base of a mountain, its walls lined with defensive formations and patrolling beasts. A four-headed serpent slithered along the parapet, its eyes scanning the swamp. A pack of stone-backed wolves patrolled the perimeter, their growls echoing through the twilight.

"Impressive," Lin said.

"Three hundred years of construction," Zhao Mei replied. "The Heavenly Sword Sect doesn't do things halfway."

They led him through the main gate, past rows of disciples who stopped to stare. Lin felt their eyes on him, their whispers following him like a shadow. The wind-snake tightened its coil, sensing his anxiety.

*Stay calm,* he told himself. *You've faced worse than this.*

But had he? He'd faced bullies and beasts, but he'd never faced an entire sect. A sect that apparently already knew about him.

They stopped at a central building—a pagoda that reached toward the sky, its roof adorned with golden tiles that caught the dying light. Jin Chen pushed open the doors, revealing a hall filled with elders.

Seven of them sat on raised platforms, their cultivation levels radiating pressure that made Lin's knees buckle. The wind-snake hissed, the lightning hawk shrieked from outside, and the shadow panther's presence flickered at the edge of his perception.

"Kneel," Jin Chen said.

Lin didn't move.

"Kneel," the elder in the center repeated. His voice was calm, but it carried the weight of absolute authority. "Or we will make you."

Lin's instincts screamed at him to obey. Every fiber of his being told him that these people could kill him without breaking a sweat. But something else—the merged power of his beasts, the resonance that connected them—whispered a different message.

*Stand. Show them you are not prey.*

Lin met the elder's eyes. "I'm not here to fight. But I won't kneel to anyone. If you have questions, ask them. If you want to accuse me, do it. But I won't bow to accusations without evidence."

The elder's eyes narrowed. For a long moment, the hall was silent. Then he laughed.

"Spine," he said. "I haven't seen spine like that in a century. Very well, Lin Xiao. We'll play this your way."

He gestured, and Jin Chen removed the binding rope. Lin rubbed his wrists, watching the elders warily.

"We know about your [Beast Resonance]," the elder continued. "We've been tracking your progress since you bonded with the wind-snake. The ability to merge with multiple beasts is unprecedented. It's also dangerous."

"Dangerous how?"

"Because you're not the only one with that ability," the elder said. "Three months ago, another tamer appeared. He called himself the Beast King. He's been collecting beasts, merging with them, and creating corrupted versions like the one you killed today. He's hunting tamers who can bond with multiple beasts. He's hunting you."

Lin's heart stopped.

"Where is he?" he asked.

"We don't know. But we have a way to find out." The elder reached into his robe and pulled out a small crystal. It pulsed with black light, the same color as the corrupted beast's qi. "This is a tracking stone. It resonates with his signature. It's been leading us to him, but every time we get close, he disappears."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you're the only one who can match him," the elder said. "Your [Beast Resonance] is the key to stopping him. But it's also the reason he wants you dead."

The crystal pulsed, then shattered.

The elder's face went pale. "He's here."

The pagoda exploded.

Stone and wood rained down as a blast of corrupted qi tore through the ceiling. Lin threw himself aside, the wind-snake's instincts guiding him to cover. The elders rose, their beasts manifesting around them in a fury of power.

And then Lin saw him.

A figure stood at the center of the destruction, surrounded by a dozen corrupted beasts. He was tall, his body covered in scars, his eyes burning with the same red light as the creatures he commanded. Black qi coiled around him like living chains.

He was smiling.

"Lin Xiao," the Beast King said. "I've been looking for you."

Lin's shadow panther materialized beside him, its growl rumbling through the debris. The lightning hawk dove through the hole in the ceiling, electricity crackling around its wings. The wind-snake hissed, venom dripping from its fangs.

"Who are you?" Lin demanded.

"I'm you," the Beast King said. "Or rather, I'm

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Price of Power

Lin's claws raked across the corrupted beast's spine, shadow-stained steel tearing through rotted flesh. The creature howled, black ichor spraying across the swamp. But instead of falling, it spun with impossible speed, its massive arm catching Lin across the chest.

He flew backward, crashing through three trees before his momentum stopped.

*"Foolish,"* the shadow panther's voice echoed in his mind. *"You fight like a beast, not a tamer. A beast dies alone."*

Lin coughed, tasting blood. The corrupted beast was already charging again, its twisted form leaving a trail of black qi that poisoned the ground beneath it. The hunting party had scattered, their so-called "elite" disciples running like frightened rabbits.

*"Then show me,"* Lin thought back.

The panther's amusement curled through their bond. *"Watch."*

The shadow panther didn't attack. It *flowed*—becoming liquid darkness that spread across the swamp floor. The corrupted beast crashed through the shadows, confused, searching for a target that no longer existed.

Then the panther struck from three directions at once.

Its claws found the creature's ankle, its knee, its throat. Not killing blows. *Debilitating* ones. The corrupted beast stumbled, its movements growing sluggish as its tendons unraveled.

*"Now,"* the panther commanded.

Lin didn't need to be told twice. He merged with the lightning hawk, becoming a bolt of living electricity that arced through the air. He struck the creature's chest, where the corruption was thickest, and *pushed*.

[Beast Resonance: Purification Protocol]

The technique wasn't something he'd learned. It was something he *remembered*—a fragment of knowledge buried deep in the resonance bond, a skill meant for cleansing tainted beasts.

White light exploded from his palms.

The corrupted beast screamed, its body convulsing as the purification energy fought against the dark qi. For a moment, Lin saw something beneath the corruption—a majestic creature, a shadow-mist tiger, trapped and suffering.

"Hold still," Lin gritted out. "I'm trying to save you."

But the corruption was too deep. It had fused with the tiger's core, becoming part of its very essence. There was no separation. There was only destruction or mercy.

The shadow panther's voice was cold. *"Kill it. Or it will kill everyone here."*

Lin looked at the hunting party. They'd stopped running, watching him with a mixture of hope and fear. Behind them, more corrupted beasts were emerging from the mist—smaller ones, but hungry.

He made his choice.

[Beast Resonance: Triple Merge - Shadow Lightning Fang]

His body became a blur of darkness and electricity. He struck the corrupted tiger's core directly, his hand punching through its chest like paper. The creature's eyes widened, recognition flickering in their depths—gratitude, even—before they went dark.

The body crumbled to ash.

Lin landed, gasping. The triple merge was draining him faster than he'd anticipated. His meridians burned, and his vision swam. He'd pushed too hard, too fast.

"Who are you?" a voice demanded.

Lin looked up. The hunting party had regrouped, their leader—a young woman with sharp features and a golden badge marking her as a core disciple of the Celestial Crane Sect—was staring at him with suspicion.

"I asked you a question," she said, her hand resting on her beast talisman. "Who are you? And what kind of technique was that?"

Lin forced himself to stand straight. "Lin Xiao. Outer disciple of the Thousand Beast Valley sect."

The woman's eyes narrowed. "Impossible. No outer disciple has that kind of power. And that technique—I've never seen anything like it. You're either a spy, or you're hiding something."

*"She's not wrong,"* the wind-snake hissed in Lin's mind. *"You're dangerous. They'll want to control you."*

Lin kept his face neutral. "I bonded with some strong beasts. Luck of the draw."

"Luck." The woman's voice dripped with disbelief. "Three beasts, one of which is a shadow panther, and you're telling me it's *luck*?"

Before Lin could respond, another disciple screamed.

They all turned. The smaller corrupted beasts had reached the edge of the clearing, their red eyes fixed on the group. There were at least a dozen of them—twisted wolves, their bodies covered in oozing sores and jagged bone protrusions.

"Form up!" the woman shouted, drawing her own beast talisman. A massive frost bear materialized beside her, its breath misting in the air. "Protect the perimeter!"

The disciples scrambled into formation, releasing their beasts—a fire fox, a stone tortoise, a wind eagle. But Lin could see the fear in their eyes. They weren't fighters. They were students, out on a routine hunting expedition that had gone terribly wrong.

*"You can't save them all,"* the shadow panther said. *"Not in your current state."*

Lin's hands trembled. The triple merge had taken too much out of him. His cultivation base was unstable, crackling with energy he couldn't fully control. If he tried to fight again, he might burn out his meridians entirely.

But he couldn't just watch them die.

"I can create an opening," he said, stepping forward. "Get your people out of here."

The woman stared at him. "You're insane. You can barely stand."

"Better insane than dead." Lin raised his hand, calling on the last dregs of his power. "On my signal, run. Don't look back."

*[Beast Resonance: Partial Merge - Shadow Mirage]*

His body dissolved into darkness, becoming a wall of shifting shadows that spread across the clearing. The corrupted wolves hesitated, confused by the sudden loss of their targets.

"Now!" Lin shouted.

The disciples ran. The woman grabbed her frost bear, casting one last look at Lin before fleeing into the mist.

The shadows held for ten seconds. Then Lin's control shattered.

He collapsed to his knees, the world spinning. The corrupted wolves turned, their attention fixed on the easy prey that had just revealed itself.

*"You're going to die,"* the lightning hawk shrieked.

*"Probably,"* Lin agreed.

The first wolf lunged.

A blur of black and white intercepted it.

The shadow panther stood over Lin, its body crackling with barely contained fury. The wolf flew backward, its neck broken before it hit the ground. The other wolves snarled, circling.

*"Get up, little tamer,"* the panther said. *"I didn't bond with you just to watch you die."*

Lin forced himself to his feet. His legs shook. His vision blurred. But he stood.

"One more," he whispered. "Just one more."

The panther's eyes gleamed. *"That's the spirit."*

They fought together—beast and tamer, their movements synchronized through the resonance bond. Lin dodged, struck, and flowed around the wolves like water. The panther covered his weaknesses, striking from angles he couldn't see, protecting his back.

Minutes passed. Or hours. Lin lost track of time.

When the last wolf fell, he collapsed, unable to move. The panther stood guard, its body covered in wounds that dripped shadow instead of blood.

*"You did well,"* the panther said, its voice softer than before. *"For a human."*

Lin laughed weakly. "High praise."

He lay there, staring at the misty sky, feeling his cultivation settle into a new equilibrium. He'd pushed past his limits and survived. His core was stronger now, more refined.

But something was wrong.

He felt it—a tugging sensation in his chest, like a thread being pulled. It came from the direction the hunting party had fled.

No.

It came from *beyond* them.

*"What is that?"* Lin asked, sitting up.

The shadow panther's ears flattened. *"Corruption. But different. Older."*

Lin's blood ran cold. He reached out with his resonance sense, probing the distance. What he found made his heart stop.

The corruption wasn't spreading from the beasts.

It was spreading from the *earth itself*.

And at its center, buried deep beneath the valley, something was waking up.

A scream echoed through the swamp—not human, not beast. It was the sound of the world crying out in pain.

Then the ground split open.

Lin barely had time to grab the panther before a chasm opened beneath them, swallowing them both into darkness.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Fifth Elder's Summons

Lin's fist connected with the corrupted beast's twisted ribcage, and the impact sent shockwaves through his merged form. Shadows rippled around his arm as the panther's power amplified the strike, lightning crackled along his forearm, and the wind-snake's venom seeped into the wound.

The creature bellowed, black blood spraying across the misty swamp.

"Again," Lin whispered, and his beasts responded as one.

The shadow panther's awareness guided his feet, allowing him to flow around the creature's thrashing claws. The lightning hawk's vision gave him perfect timing, showing him the exact moment the beast's guard dropped. The wind-snake's instincts warned him of every feint and counter.

He struck three more times in rapid succession—chest, throat, knee joint.

The corrupted beast stumbled, its body beginning to unravel. The black qi leaking from its wounds intensified, forming writhing tendrils that lashed out at everything around them.

"Get back!" Lin shouted at the pinned disciples. "It's going to explode!"

They scrambled away, robes tearing on jagged rocks, eyes wide with terror. Lin didn't wait to see if they made it. He pushed forward, driving his merged form deeper into the creature's range.

*[Beast Resonance: Shadow Lash]*

The panther's power answered his call. Dark chains erupted from Lin's shadow, wrapping around the corrupted beast's legs, anchoring it in place.

*[Beast Resonance: Thunder Talon]*

Lightning gathered in his right hand, condensing into a blade of pure electrical energy. The hawk shrieked in his mind, lending him every spark of its power.

*[Beast Resonance: Venom Fang]*

Greenish-black fluid dripped from his fingers, the wind-snake's deadliest poison coating the lightning blade.

Lin drove the combination attack into the creature's heart.

The corrupted beast froze. Its red eyes flickered, dimmed, and went dark. The black qi pouring from its wounds began to dissipate, dissolving into the mist like smoke in a storm.

For a long moment, everything was still.

Then the creature collapsed, its body crumbling into ash before it hit the ground.

Lin dropped to his knees, gasping. The triple merge released him, and he felt his beasts retreat to their spiritual spaces within his core. Exhaustion crashed over him like a wave, his muscles screaming, his meridians burning from the strain.

"That was..." a voice whispered behind him.

Lin turned his head. The disciples had stopped running. They stood at the edge of the clearing, staring at him like he was some kind of monster.

"That was impossible," another disciple finished. "You're only at the second level of the Beast Melding Realm. You shouldn't be able to do that."

"What sect are you from?" the first one demanded, stepping forward. She was tall, with sharp features and robes that marked her as a core disciple of the Celestial Crane Sect. "No outer disciple has that kind of power."

Lin forced himself to stand, swaying slightly. "I'm not from any sect."

"Impossible." The woman's eyes narrowed. "The only beast tamers in the valley belong to the five great sects or the Beast King's court. If you're not affiliated, you're a rogue."

"I'm from the valley," Lin said, his voice steady despite his exhaustion. "Born here. Raised here. Never joined a sect."

The disciples exchanged glances. Something passed between them—fear, recognition, or both.

"You're Lin Xiao," the woman said slowly. "The cripple who bonded with a broken wind-snake."

Lin's jaw tightened. "That's me."

"But you just..." She gestured at the pile of ash that had been the corrupted beast. "You just killed a Rank 5 corrupted beast. Alone. With three beasts."

"Four beasts," Lin corrected. "If you count the snake."

The woman's face went pale. She turned to her companions, and a silent conversation seemed to pass between them. Finally, she stepped forward, her posture shifting from aggressive to respectful.

"My name is Song Yuhan, core disciple of the Celestial Crane Sect. I owe you my life." She bowed deeply. "The beast that attacked us was part of a larger corrupted swarm. Our squad was sent to investigate reports of unusual activity in the eastern marshes."

Lin's tired mind latched onto the word "swarm." "How many?"

"At least fifty corrupted beasts of varying ranks. They're gathering near the old temple grounds." Song Yuhan straightened, her expression grim. "The sect elders believe someone is controlling them. Deliberately spreading the corruption to destabilize the valley."

The mist around them seemed to grow thicker, more oppressive. Lin thought about the temple he'd just left, the shadow panther's warning about the darkness spreading through the valley.

"I need to report this to my sect immediately," Song Yuhan continued. "But if you're willing to help, the Celestial Crane Sect would be grateful. We could offer you resources, protection, a position—"

"I'm not joining any sect," Lin interrupted. "But I'll help."

"Why?" The question came from one of the other disciples, a young man with suspicious eyes. "What do you get out of it?"

Lin thought about it. The truth was complicated—he didn't fully understand his own motives. But the answer came easily anyway.

"The corruption killed my parents," he said quietly. "It destroyed my village. If there's someone behind it, I want to find them. I want to stop them."

Song Yuhan nodded slowly. "Then we have common cause. But first, we need to get you somewhere safe. You're running on empty, and there are worse things than corrupted beasts in these marshes."

She was right. Lin could barely stand, and his beasts were depleted. The shadow panther had retreated deep into his spiritual space, the lightning hawk was barely a whisper, and even the wind-snake—usually so resilient—was exhausted.

"Follow me," Song Yuhan said. "There's a safe house nearby. We can rest there and plan our next move."

Lin hesitated. Trusting sect disciples went against every instinct he'd developed growing up in the valley's underbelly. But he didn't have much choice. Alone, exhausted, with depleted beasts—he was vulnerable.

"Fine," he said. "Lead the way."

The safe house was an old hunting lodge, half-sunken into the swamp but still structurally sound. Moss-covered stone walls held back the moisture, and a central fireplace provided light and warmth. The disciples settled in quickly, clearly experienced at field operations.

Lin found a corner and sat down, closing his eyes. He needed to meditate, to recover his qi and let his beasts rest. But sleep came first, pulling at him with insistent fingers.

"Before you rest," Song Yuhan said, sitting down across from him, "I need to tell you something."

Lin opened one eye. "Can it wait?"

"No." She leaned forward, her voice dropping to a whisper. "The corruption spreading through the valley—it's not natural. Someone is breeding it, cultivating it like a spirit beast. And I think I know who."

"Who?"

"The Fifth Elder of the Celestial Crane Sect."

Lin's eyes snapped open. "Your own elder?"

"Disgraced. Exiled. Ten years ago, Elder Huo was caught performing forbidden experiments—trying to merge human and beast essence. He was stripped of his rank and banished from the sect. But he never left the valley. He's been hiding in the eastern reaches, building his power."

"And you think he's behind the corruption?"

"I know he is." Song Yuhan's hands trembled, but her voice remained steady. "I was part of the team that first discovered his laboratory. We found records, journals, breeding charts. He's been creating corrupted beasts for years, testing them, refining them. And now he's ready to unleash them."

Lin's exhaustion forgotten, he sat up straight. "Where is this laboratory?"

"The old temple grounds. The same place where the swarm is gathering."

A chill ran down Lin's spine. The same temple where he'd bonded with the shadow panther. The same temple that had felt so strange and wrong, even through the bond.

"He's using the temple," Lin realized. "Using its spiritual energy to fuel his experiments."

Song Yuhan nodded. "The temple sits on a convergence of ley lines. It's one of the most spiritually active locations in the valley. If Elder Huo can corrupt that power source..."

"He could spread the corruption across the entire valley," Lin finished. "Kill everything."

"Yes."

The fire crackled. Outside, something howled in the distance—a corrupted beast, or perhaps just the wind. Lin couldn't tell anymore.

"Then we go to the temple," he said. "And we stop him."

"Not alone. Not like this." Song Yuhan reached into her robe and pulled out a small jade token, carved with the symbol of the Celestial Crane Sect. "Take this. It's a communication token, keyed to my personal frequency. Rest, recover, and when you're ready, call me. I'll gather whatever allies I can."

Lin took the token, feeling its warmth against his palm. "Why are you helping me?"

Song Yuhan smiled, but there was no humor in it. "Because you're the only beast tamer I've ever seen who can merge with multiple beasts. If anyone can stop Elder Huo, it's someone who breaks all the rules."

She stood and walked away, leaving Lin alone with his thoughts and the token in his hand.

He didn't sleep that night. Instead, he meditated, pushing his qi through his exhausted meridians, forcing his beasts to recover faster than they should. The shadow panther growled in protest but complied. The lightning hawk sent sparks of encouragement. The wind-snake coiled tighter, lending him its stubborn resilience.

By dawn, Lin felt almost human again.

He stood at the lodge's window, watching the mist rise over the swamp. Somewhere out there, a madman was building an army of corrupted beasts. Somewhere out there, the fate of the valley was being decided.

And Lin Xiao—the cripple, the laughingstock, the rogue beast tamer—was going to walk right into the heart of it.

He raised the communication token, ready to call Song Yuhan, when a knock came at the lodge's door.

Not a soft knock. A pounding, urgent and frantic.

Lin moved to answer it, but before he could reach the door, it burst open. A figure stumbled inside, bloody and broken—a disciple of the Celestial Crane Sect, her robes torn, her face a mask of terror.

"Song Yuhan!" she screamed. "They took her! The corrupted beasts—they surrounded the eastern camp—they took her!"

Lin's blood ran cold.

He looked at the communication token in his hand. Then at the terrified disciple. Then at the mist-shrouded swamp outside.

The game had changed. The enemy knew they were coming.

And now they had a hostage.

*"Interesting,"* the shadow panther's voice echoed in his mind. *"Your first real test, little tamer. What will you sacrifice to win?"*

Lin closed his hand around the token, crushing it to dust.

"I don't sacrifice anything," he said quietly. "I find another way."

He stepped past the weeping disciple and walked into the mist, his beasts stirring within him, ready for war.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Soul Eater's Bargain

The corrupted beast's claws raked through empty air where Lin had stood a heartbeat before. Shadow particles scattered like ink droplets in water, reforming twenty feet to the creature's left.

"You're slow," Lin said, his voice layered with three distinct tones—human, avian, serpentine. Lightning crackled between his fingers as he drew the beast's attention. "Is this the best corruption can offer?"

The abomination roared, black qi erupting from its wounds like volcanic ash. The hunting party screamed, scrambling further up the rock formation. One of them—a girl with crimson robes and a jade hairpin—lost her footing and slid toward the creature's feet.

Lin moved before thought could catch up. Shadow-wrapped, he appeared beneath her, catching her mid-slide. "Watch yourself," he said, depositing her back with the others. Then he was gone again, reappearing atop the creature's head.

*[Wind Blade: Serpent's Fury]*

The wind-snake's power channeled through his arm as he brought it down in a sweeping arc. Compressed air sliced through the corrupted beast's neck, severing tendons and bone. Black ichor sprayed across the clearing.

The creature stumbled, its roar turning into a gurgle. But it didn't fall.

*"The corruption runs deep,"* the shadow panther's voice whispered in his mind. *"You've only angered it."*

True enough. The beast's wounds began sealing themselves, black qi knitting flesh back together with terrifying speed. Its eyes burned brighter, fixing on Lin with undivided hatred.

"Great," Lin muttered. "It regenerates."

The lightning hawk screeched from above, diving toward the creature's eyes. Electricity surged through the abomination's skull, making it convulse. But even as it thrashed, its body continued healing.

Lin dropped back to the ground, mind racing. A normal beast, even a powerful one, would have fallen by now. But corruption didn't follow normal rules. It twisted life into something that shouldn't exist—something that refused to die.

*"Cut off the source,"* the wind-snake hissed. *"The corruption has a core. Destroy it, and the beast falls."*

"Where?" Lin asked, dodging a sweeping claw that tore furrows through the earth.

*"I don't know. But you're the Beast Resonance master. Feel it."*

Lin closed his eyes for half a second—a lifetime in combat. His senses extended through his three bonded beasts, layering their perceptions over his own. The shadow panther saw heat signatures. The lightning hawk saw elemental currents. The wind-snake smelled the corruption's trail.

And there—buried deep in the creature's chest, surrounded by layers of corrupted muscle—a pulsing knot of dark energy. Like a second heart, beating with malevolent rhythm.

"I see it," Lin breathed.

He launched himself forward, shadows trailing behind him like a cloak. The corrupted beast swung its massive arm, but Lin flowed around it, using the panther's fluid grace. He climbed the creature's body, digging his fingers into corrupted flesh, pulling himself upward.

The beast thrashed, trying to dislodge him. Black qi lashed out like tendrils, seeking to pierce his skin. One caught his shoulder, and Lin gasped as ice-cold corruption tried to worm its way into his meridians.

*[Resonance Purification]*

The wind-snake's cleansing venom surged through his channels, burning away the corruption before it could take root. His body glowed with pale green light for an instant, repelling the dark energy.

"I'm not that easy to infect," Lin growled.

He reached the creature's chest and drove his hand into the wound. His fingers closed around something solid—the corruption core. It pulsed with sickening heat, trying to wriggle free from his grasp.

*"Pull it out!"* the shadow panther urged.

Lin pulled.

The corrupted beast screamed—a sound that shook the swamp and sent birds fleeing from miles around. Black energy exploded outward, throwing Lin clear. He crashed through a tree, tumbling to a stop against a moss-covered boulder.

The core remained in his hand, pulsing weakly. The corrupted beast staggered, its body beginning to dissolve into black mist. Within seconds, nothing remained but a patch of withered ground where it had stood.

Silence fell over the swamp.

Lin lay against the boulder, gasping for breath. His body ached from the triple merge, and the corruption's touch still stung in his shoulder. But he was alive. They were all alive.

"Thank you," a voice said, shaky but sincere.

Lin looked up. The crimson-robed girl stood before him, her jade hairpin askew, her face pale. Behind her, the other disciples were slowly climbing down from the rock formation, weapons still drawn.

"I'm Lin Xiao," he said, forcing himself to stand. "What were you doing out here?"

"Hunting," the girl said. "Like you. The corruption has been spreading. Our sect sent us to investigate the source." She paused, her eyes falling on the shadow panther, which had materialized beside Lin. "You're... the Beast Resonance master. The one who bonded three beasts."

Lin tensed. "News travels fast."

"You're famous," she said, a hint of nervousness in her voice. "Or infamous, depending on who you ask. Some say you're a prodigy. Others say you're a freak who's going to burn out his soul within a year."

"The second group is louder," Lin admitted.

"I'm Su Mei," she said, extending her hand. "Third disciple of the Crimson Cloud Sect. And I think you're exactly what we need."

Lin took her hand warily. "For what?"

She glanced at the other disciples, who had formed a loose circle around them. Their faces were grim, their hands never far from their weapons.

"There's a place," Su Mei said quietly. "Deep in the swamp. The elders call it the Festering Heart. It's where the corruption first emerged, months ago. We were sent to scout it, but..." She swallowed. "We didn't make it within three miles before that thing attacked us."

"And you want me to go there?"

"We want you to help us go there," she corrected. "The corruption is spreading faster every day. If we don't find the source and destroy it, the entire valley will be infected within a year. Every beast. Every tamer. Everything."

Lin looked down at the corruption core in his hand. It had stopped pulsing, but he could still feel its malevolent energy, dormant but waiting. Waiting to spread again.

"Why me?" he asked.
